title: Odyssey, Book XI: The Descent to the Dead
description: Odysseus' nekuia — his journey to the land of the dead to consult the prophet Tiresias.
introduction: The Nekyia (Book XI of the Odyssey) is the foundational Western text on the afterlife. Odysseus sails to the edge of the world, digs a trench, pours blood offerings, and speaks with the shades of the dead. Achilles' famous declaration — that he would rather be a living serf than king of the dead — expresses the Greek view of death with devastating clarity. This passage shaped all subsequent Western conceptions of the underworld.

translation.original_text: μὴ δή μοι θάνατόν γε παραύδα, φαίδιμ᾿ Ὀδυσσεῦ. βουλοίμην κ᾿ ἐπάρουρος ἐὼν θητευέμεν ἄλλῳ, ἀνδρὶ παρ᾿ ἀκλήρῳ, ᾧ μὴ βίοτος πολὺς εἴη, ἢ πᾶσιν νεκύεσσι καταφθιμένοισιν ἀνάσσειν.
translation.text: Do not try to console me for death, glorious Odysseus. I would rather be a serf laboring for another man — a man without a plot of land, without much to live on — than be king over all the wasted dead.

translation.original_text: αὕτη δίκη ἐστὶ βροτῶν, ὅτε τίς κε θάνῃσιν· οὐ γὰρ ἔτι σάρκας τε καὶ ὀστέα ἶνες ἔχουσιν, ἀλλὰ τὰ μέν τε πυρὸς κρατερὸν μένος αἰθομένοιο δαμνᾷ.
translation.text: This is the way of mortals when they die: the sinews no longer hold the flesh and the bones together, but the fierce power of blazing fire destroys them.
